question:
Refer to Examples 12.1-12.3, in which an antique collector modeled the auction price $(y)$ of grandfather clocks as a function of the age of the clock $\left(x_1ight)$ and the number of bidders ( $x_2$ ). Recall that the hypothesized first-order model is

$$
y= eta_0+ eta_1 x_1+ eta_2 x_2+\varepsilon
$$


The SPSS printout for the analysis is shown in Figure 12.6.
a. Find and interpret the adjusted coefficient of determination $R_{\mathrm{a}}^2$.
b. Conduct the global $F$-test of model usefulness at the $\alpha=.05$ level of significance.
answer:
a. The $R_{\mathrm{a}}^2$ value (highlighted in Figure 12.6) is .885 . This implies that the least squares model has explained about $88.5 \%$ of the total sample variation in $y$ values (auction prices), after adjusting for sample size and number of independent variables in the model.
b. The elements of the global test of the model follow:
$H_0:  eta_1= eta_2=0($ Note: $k=2)$
$H_a$ : At least one of the two model coefficients, $ eta_1$ and $ eta_2$, is nonzero.
Test statistic: $F= rac{	ext { MS }(	ext { Model) }}{	ext { MSE }}= rac{2,141,531}{17,818}=120.19$ (see Figure 12.6)
$p$-value $\approx 0$
Conclusion: Because $\alpha=.05$ exceeds the observed significance level, $(p \approx 0)$, the data provide strong evidence that at least one of the model coefficients is nonzero. The overall model appears to be statistically useful for predicting auction prices.

meta_info.caption: Assessing Overall Adequacy-Clock Auction Price Model
